subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Why Is There Something Rather Than Nothing?
description: A micro-lesson that makes Why Is There Something Rather Than Nothing? usable.

hook.text: The Big Bang should have created equal parts matter and antimatter—which would annihilate, leaving nothing.

buildup.text: But a tiny imbalance meant for every billion antimatter particles, there were a billion and one matter particles.

discovery.text: That one-in-a-billion surplus is everything—every galaxy, star, planet, and person. The rest annihilated.

twist.text: We don't know what caused the asymmetry. It's one of the deepest unsolved problems in physics.

climax.text: Experiments at CERN search for differences between matter and antimatter behavior that could explain the imbalance.

punchline.text: One extra particle per billion. That tiny surplus is everything.

quiz.question: Why does matter exist at all after the Big Bang?
options: A tiny asymmetry produced slightly more matter than antimatter
The Big Bang only created matter, not antimatter
Dark energy prevented antimatter from forming
